---
title: "Day 2 (Tuesday): Trees and Modern Pipelines"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, ../assets/css/slides-theme.scss]
    slide-number: true
    incremental: true
    scrollable: true
    slide-level: 2
---

## Learning objectives

- Contrast two traditions: **write-down models** vs **flexible prediction** — both need honest checking on new data.
- Build intuition for **decision trees** (sequences of simple “if–then” rules on measurements).
- Use R’s **`tidymodels`** tools to **bundle preprocessing, fitting, and scoring** so comparisons stay fair (builds on **`lm`** / **`glm`**).

## Navigation

- [Teaching Hub](../index.qmd)
- [Curriculum Outline](../curriculum-outline.qmd)
- [Module Library](../modules/index.qmd)
- [Back: Monday deck](day-01-monday.qmd)
- [Next: Thursday deck](day-03-thursday.qmd)

## Two cultures framing

- **Classical / generative view:** start with assumptions you can defend, then quantify uncertainty (close to how many of you were taught statistics).
- **Algorithmic / predictive view:** start with flexible patterns, then worry hard about **error on new data** (common in ML competitions).
- Good applied work **mixes both**: clear measurements + careful evaluation.

## Why trees now?

- Trees model nonlinear effects and interactions naturally
- Easy to explain split logic to students
- Great bridge from parametric models to ensembles

## Decision tree mechanics

- Recursive partitioning of feature space
- Impurity criteria (Gini, entropy)
- Stopping rules and pruning to control complexity

## Demo: tree splitting intuition

- Optional: draw one 2D partition by hand or use a single `rpart` plot in the console; full coding practice belongs in exercises.

## Pipeline mindset

- **Reproducibility** and **leakage** first: if a step “learns” from the data (means, categories, PCs), it must be repeated correctly inside each train/test split.
- Treat **cleaning and scaling** as part of the model, not something you do once by hand and forget.
- **Decide how you will score mistakes** before you hunt for the best tuning knobs.

## Tuesday roadmap (then the R package names)

- **Split rows** so some penguins are only used to *check* performance, never to *choose* settings dishonestly.
- **Write preprocessing once** (e.g. turn factors into 0/1 columns, scale bill length) and apply the same rules on each split.
- **Name the model family** (here: classification tree) **before** fitting — including which settings you will try later.
- **Glue preprocessing + model** into one object so the computer resamples the **whole** process, not half of it.

## Split your training and test rows (`rsample`)

- **Goal — hide future rows from past rows:** `initial_split(data, prop = 0.8, strata = outcome)` records which rows are for **training** vs **testing** (an `rsplit` object in code).
- `training(split)` and `testing(split)` materialize the two tibbles. **Never** preprocess the full dataset before splitting if steps learn from data (means, factor levels, PCA loadings).
- For repeated estimates of generalization error, use `vfold_cv(training(split), v = 10, strata = outcome)` or nested resampling when tuning within an outer loop.

## Preprocessing as a reusable checklist (`recipes`)

- **Goal — a reusable checklist of preprocessing:** `recipe(outcome ~ predictors, data = train_data) |> step_*()` lists steps in order.
- **Roles:** `all_numeric_predictors()`, `all_nominal_predictors()`, `all_outcomes()` select columns by role instead of fragile name vectors.
- Common steps: `step_impute_*`, `step_normalize`, `step_dummy`, `step_zv` (zero-variance), `step_corr` (remove redundant columns), `step_log`, `step_ns` (splines).
- `prep(recipe, training = train_data)` learns constants from training rows; `bake(prep, new_data = ...)` applies the same rules to validation/test rows. This is what prevents leakage.

## Name the model family before you fit (`parsnip`)

- **Goal — separate “what model?” from “fit it now”:** the **specification** names the family and unknown settings (example in slides: penalized logistic with `glmnet` engine).
- `set_engine()` picks the computational backend (`"glmnet"`, `"ranger"`, `"xgboost"`, `"keras"`). Same user-facing call can map to different packages.
- `translate()` shows how the **same R call** maps to the **underlying package** (handy when a blog post uses `xgboost` arguments verbatim).
- **Fit** happens later via `fit(workflow, data = train_data)` or `last_fit`, after workflows bundle preprocessing + model.

## One object that runs preprocessing + model together (`workflows`)

- **Goal — one object to resample:** `workflow() |> add_recipe(rec) |> add_model(mod)` packages preprocessing + model so cross-validation always runs both.
- Benefits: the resampler sees the *whole* estimation chain; tuning grids can include recipe parameters (e.g. spline degrees) alongside model hyperparameters.
- `workflow() |> add_formula(y ~ .) |> add_model(mod)` skips a recipe when you only need a bare formula, but recipes remain the right place for learned steps.

## Try settings honestly; score with the same metrics (`tune`, `dials`, `yardstick`)

- **Goal — try several settings honestly:** mark unknowns with `tune()`, build a candidate grid, and score each with the **same** metric set (e.g. ROC AUC + sensitivity) on held-out folds.
- `extract_parameter_set_dials(wf)` + `grid_regular()` / `grid_space_filling()` build candidate grids; `tune_grid(wf, resamples = folds, grid = ..., metrics = metric_set(roc_auc, pr_auc))` evaluates them.
- `collect_metrics()`, `autoplot()`, and `select_best()` summarize results; `finalize_workflow(wf, best_params)` plugs in winners.
- `yardstick::metric_set` keeps evaluation consistent across models; choose metrics **before** peeking at test data.

## Tidymodels blueprint (overview)

- **Preprocessing steps** (`recipes`) — impute, encode, normalize, and the like
- **Model specification** (`parsnip`) — choose the *family* before fitting
- **Workflow** (`workflows`) — bind preprocessing + model so resampling sees both
- **Splits + tuning** (`rsample`, `tune`) — cross-validation and hyperparameter search

## Mental checklist before you `fit`

1. Decide how you will **split** and (if needed) **cross-validate**.
2. Build preprocessing on **training** rows only; check each step makes scientific sense.
3. Pick the **model family** + software **engine**; wrap in a **workflow**.
4. Pick **scores** that match the biology or clinic question; tune if needed.
5. Fit the final workflow on **all training data**; assess **once** on the held-out test set (or use nested CV for serious tuning comparisons).

## Reusable classification template

- One recipe skeleton
- One resampling object
- Plug-in model specs (tree, forest, boosting, neural net)
- Comparable metrics and tuning framework

## Shared pipeline template (single source)

The **next slides** are a **slow walk-through** of the same **Adelie vs Gentoo** penguin example as [Module 04](../modules/module-04-pipeline.qmd) and the [classification notebook](../notebooks/penguins-classification.Rmd): each stage has a **short intuition slide**, then **R code** (shared file used here and in Module 04), then **four figure** slides. Say out loud: in research you keep a final **test** set; some plots here reuse **fit rows** for speed. For **sex** or **three-species** notebooks, see the **Data** page.

{{< include ../_includes/tidymodels-pipeline.qmd >}}

## If you already know `caret` (optional bridge)

- Older **`caret::train()`** often mixed **preprocessing**, **model choice**, and **resampling** in one call; **`tidymodels`** splits those into clearer pieces so cross-validation cannot accidentally skip a step.
- Rough map: training control \(\rightarrow\) `control_*()` helpers; method string \(\rightarrow\) model spec + `set_engine()`; `preProcess` \(\rightarrow\) recipe steps; variable importance \(\rightarrow\) `vip` or engine-specific summaries.
- The payoff is the same idea as good lab practice: **every transformation is scripted and replayed** the same way on each fold.

## Comparing many models fairly (`workflowsets`, `stacks`)

- **`workflowsets`** — run **several** preprocessing + model combinations on the **same** cross-validated folds (fair homework comparisons).
- **`stacks`** — treat a few tuned models as **candidates**, then learn a **simple combination** (meta-learner) on top — a nice capstone after forests and boosting.
- Pedagogy: introduce **one** workflowset example after students are comfortable with a **single** workflow.

## Transition to Thursday

- Tuesday gives the pipeline backbone
- Thursday swaps in stronger learners and richer evaluation
- Exercises are handled separately
